item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one, any addition to the sarkic lore is a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The cylinder at the end didn't really give any more meaningful information than we had with the historical record, and continues the trend of "note lying around the anomaly for some reason." Still, it was done well enough that I didn't mind, and the article proper was a lot of fun. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because there is no evidence that it bears the same magnitude as in the myths they inspired, and reaching conclusions only based on mythical evidence seems like something off for the Foundation for me.

A lot of anomalies hint to ancient myths, and end up being really dangerous. But others end up being just inspiration for legends far greater than the truth. And the Foundation frowns upon superstition replacing science on the investigation. By the way, several of the tales about myths being true play on this, making the Foundation take the long road to reach the same conclusions, sometimes looking as fools for their lack of faith or respect for old traditions, sometimes as wise people who come to an even better understanding than what was passed down by legend.

Either way, as much as the relation with the Two Giants is already referred, at least they could explicitly put there that they're taking an extra measure of precaution because of the myths. You could either opt to make the danger seem great because of hints that the eschatological event is bound to happen, or small because of evidence that it won't be as huge a role as Yajuj and Majuj have in myth, but better safe than sorry.

In the end, it is of little concern. To me, at least, the idea of this anomaly inspiring the legends and what its investigation tells of the past is far more relevant than any reference to the events of prophecies being an actual possibility. It was a very good ride. And in-universe containment would still be a concern not because of any K-Class scenario, but simply because of the cost and damage to be expected from two anomalous armies from an ancient war wandering around in full Sarkic glory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is an accurate description of the anomaly itself, but as with many of my articles, it exists in a larger cultural context. SCP-2309 is portrayed as the origin of the concept of Yajuj and Majuj/Gog and Magog, which play important roles in the eschatological narratives in the Quran/Book of Revelation. A recurring theme in the SCP universe is that there are a lot of hidden truths to be found in the Abrahamic scriptures… why wouldn't that also be the case here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only qualm here is that I don't think the Foundation would put so much emphasis on a possible SK Scenario here. As far as this anomaly goes, it is potentially dangerous, sure, specially to secrecy, but it is of little risk to baseline humanity.

It is just one of the armies Adytum had in the past, and was being held at bay by an ancient common army before the wall was erected. There is no reason to imagine it as being a particularly worrisome troop of sarkicists, all things considered. Just think about this army reaping through the wall, just to finally find out that their empire is no more and the heathens live in the modern world.

And I don't think it should be more dangerous. What makes it interesting is the idea of an ancient sarkicist army turned into a living fragment of the past.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sarkic Mythos and the Abrahamic faiths are my favorite SCPs (followed closely by anything lovecraftian in nature).

Brining the two together just really does it for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is in fact spelled Yaldabaoth. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dang, how'd I miss this one? :O Great historical context, and did you link the Daevites to Zoroastrianism? Neat.

(Isn't it "Yaldabaoth"? Not exactly easy to spell, but…)

item.title: Reverted an edit to this article
item.content:encoded: I reverted a recent edit that placed all of the footnotes after the punctuation marks, instead of after. Though I concede that the MLA style guide suggests putting footnotes after punctuation, my person preference is to place them before for aesthetic reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it depends on which faction you're talking about, and also what definition of worship you're using. They do revere Yaldaboth as much as they can be said to revere anything, if for no other reason than that it is both the source of their power and the ideal state to which they aspire. Ion would be more like their Buddha or Jesus; their great teacher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only complaint is that sarkicists do not worship Yaldabaoth, seeing it as subservient to Ion.

Decibelle beat me to the Trump jokes. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Plus all the ones. This is an excellent addition to the Sarkic mythos. There's some wonderful imagry with the wall and the hordes affect on it, as well as the depiction of the horde itself. The fact that these horrible things are swarming all around the area, yet it takes a particular method to percieve them is reminiscent of 2480.

Eta: Facepalm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice indeed! I particularly like the narration of how the Demon Hordes were only visible when the 'Magian' was touching the writer. Also, knowing that the Caliph Wathik was the inspiration for Beckford's "Vathek" added a nice touch.

Definitely a plus vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I won't deny that I did a lot of name-dropping, especially towards the end, since I was trying to emulate the style of the Cyrus Cylinder, which does quite a lot name-dropping itself. This article does use a lot of religious terminology, but I think that it is very important for verisimilitude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The inclusion of Cyrus the Great isn't only a good tie-in, it's a great way to lend legitimacy to this article, which admittedly I was a bit skeptical of at first. But, the historical texts are actually put together pretty believably, and written extremely well content-wise. This could have been easily dismissed as a cliche "demon army" but the history behind it definitely lends interest beyond that.

As a Historian, it's clear to me that you try to name-drop as much as possible. Fortunately, however, it all comes together quite cohesively in the end. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very cool. I've been fascinated with Zoroastrianism for much of my life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Indeed. And I will note that these guys are not the conventional Proto-Sarkicists or SCP-2478 who meekly pray for their saviour Ion. And neither are they the conventional Neo-Sarkicists who wish to supplant Ion to empower themselves.

These guys are essentially battle-hardened veterans from the Sarkic-Mekhanist conflict of the late Bronze Age. Men, women, things, whatever they are, who are all loyal to the original Sarkic cause as laid out by Ion himself.

In retrospect, this article added something new to the table.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that I think about it, the containment this SCP provides is very good and it is unlikely the Karcists can breach it from within. It is unlike that there will be a breach, but we can never be too careful about these matters.

That's the problem, though. The Sarkic army behind this wall has had over 2700 years to develop and improve its forces… what will they look once they do manage to breach it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Univine, you are welcome. I like the improvements you have conceived of, and Cyrus the Great is a good tie-in.

Because of the eschatological significance assigned to SCP-2309, and the existence of written and archeological evidence suggesting that SCP-2309 was constructed as a barrier against Sarkic armies, it is believed that the breach of SCP-2309, if and when it should take place, may constitute an SK-Class dominance shift scenario. Historical and esoteric research regarding the nature of the threat posed by SCP-2309 and possible improvements to its containment is ongoing.

Now that I think about it, the containment this SCP provides is very good and it is unlikely the Karcists can breach it from within. It is unlike that there will be a breach, but we can never be too careful about these matters.

item.title: Thanks to my reviewer
item.content:encoded: My thanks to MrWrong, who helped critique this piece on forums and led me to develop this idea much further than what I had intended when I first wrote the draft.
